techniques.evidence_quote: the multi-factor code had changed when they tried to log into their AWS account last week

all their Electric Compute Cloud (EC2) services, which let clients access virtual computers to run their applications, were deleted... the attackers destroyed critical data on the company's servers, including the app code and user information such as names, mailing addresses and payment details.
The company's app is online but cannot process orders

attack_chain.description: A disgruntled former employee (reportedly named Lava Kumar, laid off prior to the incident) used retained credentials to access KiranaPro's root AWS account and GitHub account. Credentials were never revoked after the employee's departure.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the hacking happened after someone gained access to their systems via a former employee's account

attack_chain.description: The attacker modified or replaced the MFA (Google Authenticator) configuration on the AWS root account, locking out legitimate owners and entrenching their own access.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the multi-factor code had changed when they tried to log into their AWS account last week

attack_chain.description: Using root-level AWS access, the attacker deleted all EC2 instances hosting the application and its data, including app source code and customer records (names, addresses, payment details). GitHub repositories were also wiped.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: all their Electric Compute Cloud (EC2) services, which let clients access virtual computers to run their applications, were deleted... the attackers destroyed critical data on the company's servers, including the app code and user information such as names, mailing addresses and payment details.

attack_chain.description: As a direct consequence of the EC2 deletion, KiranaPro's grocery delivery app became unable to process orders, effectively halting all business operations.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The company's app is online but cannot process orders

initial_access_vector: Former employee retained valid cloud credentials (AWS root account and GitHub) after being laid off; credentials were never revoked, enabling direct login without any exploitation of external vulnerabilities.
persistence_mechanism: Attacker modified the AWS root account's MFA (Google Authenticator) configuration, changing the TOTP seed and locking out legitimate account owners, thereby entrenching exclusive access.
privilege_escalation: No escalation required — the former employee already possessed root-level AWS credentials from their time of employment, granting the highest possible privilege tier from the outset.
